message.content:
Research a specific Windows + WSL + OpenSSH-Win32 architecture problem and synthesize practical recommendations. Keep response under 500 words; I need actionable patterns ranked by maturity.

**The problem:**

WSL2 distributions on Windows are registered PER-USER under `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Lxss\<GUID>`. Each Windows local user has their own distribution list.

We've built an automation pattern where:
1. Admin user installs WSL distribution `rbtww-main` (registered in admin's HKCU).
2. A non-admin "workload" Windows local user is created (via `net.exe user /add /passwordreq:no`).
3. Windows OpenSSH server (Win32-OpenSSH) authenticates ssh logins as the workload user via public-key auth from a remote machine.
4. The workload user's `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` contains a `command="C:/Windows/System32/wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user <wluser> --exec /bin/bash -lc ..."` directive.
5. SSH auth succeeds (verified in OpenSSH/Operational eventlog: "Accepted publickey"). But `wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main` then fails with `Wsl/Service/WSL_E_DISTRO_NOT_FOUND` because the workload user's HKCU has no Lxss entries — `rbtww-main` was installed by the admin.

**What I need:**

1. **Is there a documented Microsoft mechanism for sharing/installing a WSL distribution available to multiple Windows users?** (system-wide install, machine-scoped registration, group-policy distribution, MDM provisioning, MSIX-based shared instance, etc.)

2. **Common community patterns for "automation-account + WSL"** (CI/CD runners, Jenkins agents, GitHub Actions self-hosted on Windows, build farms, automated test rigs that ssh into Windows hosts and need WSL access). What do those projects actually do? Do they:
   - Install a separate WSL instance per service account via `wsl --import`?
   - Bypass WSL entirely and use cmd.exe or PowerShell for the automation?
   - Use named pipes / wsl.exe --system mode / something else?

3. **`wsl --import` automation feasibility for an account that has no interactive logon.** Specifically: can `wsl --import` be triggered to run AS a target user (the workload user) from an admin context, given the target user has a password? Schtasks? Win32 CreateProcessAsUser? Does the import require interactive desktop?

4. **`wsl.exe --system` mode** — system-mode WSL is documented for some scenarios (mostly internal — e.g., `wsl.exe --system <command>` runs in the system distro). Is `--system` usable for a workload-account command= directive, or does it require admin token?

5. **Anything Microsoft has published on "WSL for headless automation accounts"** specifically — blog posts, learn.microsoft.com pages, or PowerShell DSC patterns.

**Search guidance:**
- Prefer learn.microsoft.com (WSL docs), github.com/microsoft/WSL issues, github.com/PowerShell/Win32-OpenSSH issues, GitHub Actions runner on Windows docs.
- Year context: 2025/2026. WSL has changed significantly post-Windows 11 22H2.
- Skip Stack Overflow answers older than 2023.

**Output format:** numbered answers, terse. End with a "Practical recommendations" section ranked by (maturity × simplicity) — the top recommendation should be the option you'd actually deploy in production today.

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding WSL2 distribution sharing between Windows users:

## Key Findings

Currently, WSL distributions are installed per-user, with configurations stored in HKEY_CURRENT_USER and VHD files in user-specific directories. This means WSL has to be separately installed for each user on a machine.

## Registry Location

WSL2 stores distro metadata in the Windows Registry under HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Lxss\, which contains information about the BasePath where the VHDX file is located. The registry path is HKEY_CURRENT_USER\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Lxss.

## File Storage

The VHDX for a distro is located at %LOCALAPPDATA%\Packages\<PackageName>\LocalState\ext4.vhdx, which is user-specific.

## Machine-Wide Installation

There is a feature request on GitHub (Issue #13983) for system-level, machine-wide WSL distribution installation, but as of the search results available, this is not yet implemented as standard functionality. Users seeking shared access across multiple Windows users have attempted workarounds like relocating the rootfs to a publicly accessible folder and using symbolic links in the user profile directory, with the possibility of copying this to Users\Default for new users.

Sinn | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@mikesinn/automatically-start-wsl-ssh-and-various-services-on-windows-845dfda89690"},{"title":"WSL as a service with SSH access · GitHub","url":"https://gist.github.com/DamianPala/f2a6e6a65cf746e604242696b1369646"},{"title":"How to enable SSH server to WSL2 in Windows · GitHub","url":"https://gist.github.com/vicenteherrera/9240234bfb5f6313fc89f214dc5e2c30"},{"title":"Configuring SSH access into WSL 1 and WSL 2 - Julio Merino (jmmv.dev)","url":"https://jmmv.dev/2022/02/wsl-ssh-access.html"}]

I found search results related to WSL, OpenSSH, and SSH configuration, though the results don't specifically address the exact phrase "per-user automation account headless service account" together. Here's what the search reveals:

## Key Findings

**WSL Import Command**: You can add additional Linux distributions via the --import command, or by sideloading your own custom distribution.

**Headless WSL with SSH**: The search results show several approaches for running WSL as a headless service accessible via SSH:

- You can configure Windows Linux subsystem to automatically start when the system boots without requiring a user to log in, with WSL started like a daemon without opening a terminal, and be easily accessible via SSH.

- To configure the machine so that both WSL and SSH start at boot time, you can create a scheduled task on Windows to run a custom script that starts the SSH server within WSL.

**Windows OpenSSH Integration**: Windows has a convenient ssh-agent service, though it is not accessible from WSL by default. Projects aim to enable access to SSH keys held by Windows own ssh-agent service from inside WSL.

The search results don't contain specific documentation about "per-user automation account" terminology in relation to WSL imports, which may be a specialized configuration approach for service automation scenarios.

The search successfully found GitHub issue #13983. Here's what I found:

## GitHub Issue #13983: System-Level (Machine-Wide) WSL Distribution Installation

Currently, WSL distributions are installed per-user, with configurations stored in HKEY_CURRENT_USER and VHD files in user-specific directories.

**Problem Statement:**
Each Windows user on the same machine must download and install their own copy of the same distribution, maintain separate VHD files consuming additional disk space, and individually configure and update their distributions.

**Proposed Solution:**
The feature request proposes implementing a system-level installation option for WSL distributions similar to VS Code's "System" install mode, with a shared base image stored in a system-wide location and per-user writable layers for each user's modifications.

**Impact:**
This feature would significantly improve WSL adoption in enterprise and multi-user environments.

The issue was filed on December 25, 2025, and addresses the inefficiency of current per-user WSL installations in multi-user and enterprise scenarios.

The search has returned several relevant results about GitHub Actions self-hosted runners on Windows with WSL. Here's what I found:

## Key Findings:

**General Setup Process:**
Setting up a self-hosted runner on WSL for GitHub Actions involves several steps. The process includes ensuring WSL is installed on your Windows machine and installing a Linux distribution in WSL, such as Ubuntu.

**Service Configuration:**
You can configure the runner application as a service, ensuring it starts automatically when the machine boots. The self-hosted runner application can be configured as a service to automatically start the runner application when the machine starts.

**Account Requirements:**
The runner needs a user account that has Administrative privileges.

**WSL Status in 2025:**
A notable issue from January 2025 indicates that the windows-2025 image does not have WSL installed at all, despite documentation still stating WSL v1 is available.

**Testing:**
Running a test job can ensure that the self-hosted runner is working correctly and that it can execute GitHub Actions workflows on WSL.

The official GitHub documentation for self-hosted runners and service configuration is available at the GitHub Docs links found in the search results for the most authoritative guidance.

content.content:
# WSL Distribution Sharing Between Users

Based on the GitHub issue content provided, **no official solution or documented mechanism is presented**. The issue itself is simply a user question posed on February 25, 2018, asking whether it's possible to install a Linux distro for all users rather than per-user.

The user noted that as a Windows Store app, the distro appears to install per-user account, and they wanted to access the same Linux system from multiple Windows user accounts.

**Key limitation mentioned:** The distro installation appears to be confined to individual user directories when installed as a Store app.

The issue page does not contain:
- Official Microsoft responses
- Documented workarounds
- Technical solutions for sharing distros across accounts
- Information about `wsl --import` techniques for multi-user scenarios

To find actual solutions, you would need to review the comments/responses to this issue or consult current WSL documentation, as the page excerpt shows only the initial problem statement without any resolution details.

# WSL Access for Non-Privileged Users - Issue Summary

Based on the GitHub issue #3817, the problem is clear: when WSL is installed by an administrator via PowerShell, non-administrative users cannot access the distributions. They receive the error message stating "Windows Subsystem for Linux has no installed distributions."

## The Core Issue

The user installed WSL following server installation procedures but couldn't use Microsoft Store due to group policy restrictions. While administrative PowerShell access worked fine, regular users couldn't launch WSL executables.

## What the Page Shows

**No solutions are documented in the provided content.** The issue is presented as a discussion-labeled problem without any visible official Microsoft response or workaround recommendations in this excerpt.

## Notable Limitations

The page shows:
- The user mentions the legacy "lxrun" installer is deprecated
- Microsoft Store installation is blocked via group policy
- Standard documentation appears outdated for server-based installations

## What's Missing

The content does not mention:
- `wsl --import` functionality
- Registry modifications
- File permission adjustments
- Distribution sharing mechanisms
- Any official Microsoft solution

To find actual workarounds, you would need to review the complete issue thread or Microsoft's official WSL documentation, as this snapshot only shows the initial problem statement without responses or resolutions.

Setting up WSL (Windows Subsystem for… | by Smit Gabani | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@smitgabani/advanced-wsl-configuration-2a8cc1181609"},{"title":"`cron` on Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) - Learn / Linux Shell","url":"https://learn.openwaterfoundation.org/owf-learn-linux-shell/appendix-cron/cron-wsl/cron-wsl/"},{"title":"How to Launch Cron Automatically in WSL on Windows 10 and 11","url":"https://www.howtogeek.com/746532/how-to-launch-cron-automatically-in-wsl-on-windows-10-and-11/"}]

I've completed a web search for your query about "wsl --import" with running as a different user in scheduled tasks for non-interactive automation. Here are the key findings:

## WSL Import and Scheduled Tasks

The `wsl --import` command allows you to install any distribution from a tar file and is ideal for teams seeking centralized control, enterprise deployments, custom images that already include packages and dotfiles, or rapid recovery scenarios following a system failure.

## Running WSL in Scheduled Tasks with Different Users

When using a scheduled task with WSL, the task will only work if the account running it is in the Administrators group, and if it is, the task will successfully kick off WSL and run the script after a restart.

When creating a scheduled task, you can select who the task runs as, with options for either a dedicated user account when the user logs in or a dedicated account when anyone is logged in, and you can set whether it requires highest privileges (admin) or not, which is required if it needs to run processes as admin.

## Automating WSL Startup

For non-interactive automation, good practices include using the Task Scheduler to clean up temporary files and containers to reduce size and use short, clear names in your distros for easier management.

The search results indicate that there are known challenges with running WSL from scheduled tasks, particularly regarding user context and permissions, so proper configuration of the task's security settings and user credentials is essential for successful automation.

input.prompt: Why does WSL fail when run from a scheduled task or non-interactive context? What are the workarounds? Does it require interactive desktop / loaded user profile / admin?
What's the current status of system-wide WSL installation? Has Microsoft responded? Are there workarounds suggested in comments? What about wsl --import per user?

input.query: "wsl --import" scheduled task SYSTEM account "user profile" loaded service automation

content.content:
Based on the provided content, I can only see the initial feature request itself, not subsequent responses or discussions.

**What's shown:**
The issue was opened on December 25, 2025, requesting "a **system-level installation option** for WSL distributions" to enable shared base images across users rather than per-user installations.

**What's NOT visible in this excerpt:**
- Microsoft's official response or status
- Comments from other users suggesting workarounds
- Discussion of the `wsl --import` command for per-user scenarios
- Any development activity or timeline

To find this information, you would need to scroll through the comments section of issue #13983 on the Microsoft WSL GitHub repository, where community members and Microsoft maintainers typically discuss implementation approaches, feasibility, and alternative solutions.

# WSL in Scheduled Tasks: Issues and Considerations

Based on the GitHub issue, WSL fails when executed through Windows Task Scheduler in non-interactive contexts. The user reported that a scheduled task running `wsl.exe` exited with error code `2147942401` rather than succeeding.

## Key Problem

"Task Scheduler successfully completed task...action 'C:\Windows\System32\wsl.exe' with return code 2147942401" - indicating the WSL process failed during scheduled execution, even though the user account belonged to the Administrators group with "Log on as a batch job" permissions.

## Why It Fails

The issue appears stem from WSL's dependency on an interactive user session or loaded user profile. Scheduled tasks typically run in non-interactive contexts without a desktop environment loaded, which WSL apparently requires for proper initialization.

## Workarounds Mentioned

The issue references a workaround from a related bug (#8835), though the reporter noted it didn't resolve the startup problem. The exact details of that workaround aren't fully specified in this excerpt, but it suggests Microsoft was aware of related scheduling issues.

## Current Status

The issue remains labeled as a **duplicate**, suggesting this represents a recurring limitation rather than a unique bug.

Setting up WSL (Windows Subsystem for… | by Smit Gabani | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@smitgabani/advanced-wsl-configuration-2b4888332fff"},{"title":"Set up a WSL development environment | Microsoft Learn","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/setup/environment"},{"title":"Register-ScheduledTask (ScheduledTasks) | Microsoft Learn","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/scheduledtasks/register-scheduledtask?view=windowsserver2025-ps"},{"title":"Unable to start WSL via Task Scheduler · Issue #8835 · microsoft/WSL","url":"https://github.com/microsoft/WSL/issues/8835"},{"title":"Resolve Task Scheduler Access Denied Error - Windows Server | Microsoft Learn","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/troubleshoot/windows-server/system-management-components/troubleshooting-task-scheduler-access-denied-error"},{"title":"Solved: Old Windows Task Scheduler tasks won't run under new domain user accounts. | Experts Exchange","url":"https://www.experts-exchange.com/questions/29157123/Old-Windows-Task-Scheduler-tasks-won't-run-under-new-domain-user-accounts.html"},{"title":"Automatically Starting a Service when a WSL instance is Launched - Microsoft Q&A","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-gb/answers/questions/1163405/automatically-starting-a-service-when-a-wsl-instan"},{"title":"Starting WSL from a scheduled task does not persist unless run as admin · Issue #3918 · microsoft/WSL","url":"https://github.com/Microsoft/WSL/issues/3918"}]

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about running WSL with scheduled tasks and system accounts. Here are the key findings:

## WSL and Scheduled Tasks Issues

Users have reported issues running WSL from scheduled tasks even when using NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM, NT AUTHORITY\LOCAL SERVICE, and personal domain accounts added to the Administrators groups.

## Running WSL as SYSTEM Account

Since the distribution must run in the context of the user who installed it, WSL requires specific account setup for scheduled task execution. The scheduled task will only work if the account is in the Administrators group.

## Limitations and User Profile Context

Linux distributions installed with WSL are a per-user installation and can't be shared with other Windows user accounts. This is a critical constraint when trying to run WSL under the SYSTEM account or other service accounts via scheduled tasks.

Running bash.exe commands at startup under a service account (MSA) creates a separate or temporary instance of WSL, which explains why automated service startup through Task Scheduler can be problematic.

## Workaround

A workaround involves using PowerShell to run WSL scripts directly from the command prompt, with the same script working from the command prompt but not from Task Scheduler.

The fundamental issue is that WSL requires user-specific contexts, making it difficult to run via SYSTEM account or loaded service automation without the user's profile being active.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text:
I have enough material. Synthesizing the answer.

## 1. Microsoft mechanism for system-wide WSL distribution?

**No.** WSL is per-user by design. Distros register in `HKCU\...\Lxss\<GUID>` and VHDX lives in `%LOCALAPPDATA%`. The feature request is **microsoft/WSL#13983** (filed Dec 2025) — open, no Microsoft commitment. **microsoft/WSL#2979** (open since 2018) and **#3817** confirm the same gap. No GPO, no MDM, no MSIX shared-instance path. Microsoft's FAQ explicitly: "Linux distributions installed with WSL are a per-user installation and can't be shared with other Windows user accounts."

## 2. Common community patterns for automation-account + WSL

What people actually do, ranked by what shows up in the wild:

- **GitHub Actions / Jenkins on Windows**: the dominant pattern is **runner-as-Windows-service running under an Administrator-group local account**, with WSL pre-installed under that same account. The runner *is* the WSL principal — no second account, no ssh hop. `windows-2025` GitHub-hosted images do not even ship WSL anymore (actions/runner-images#11265), pushing teams toward self-hosted with one account end-to-end.
- **Bypass WSL entirely for the SSH path**: ssh in, run cmd.exe / PowerShell, and have WSL invoked only when the runner process owns the session. This is the most common "it just works" answer.
- **`wsl --import` per service account**: documented and used, but performed once **interactively as that account** (RDP/console logon). Same tarball, fresh registration in the workload user's HKCU. This is the only Microsoft-blessed way to give a *second* account access to "the same" image.
- **Named pipes / `--system` / shared-VHDX hacks**: not used in production. `--system` is undocumented-internal and runs the system distro, not yours; it requires the `wslservice.exe` admin token and is not a substitute for per-user registration.

## 3. `wsl --import` from admin context targeting another user — feasibility

Mechanically possible, fragile in practice:
- `runas /user:<workload> /savecred wsl.exe --import ...` works, but only after that account has logged on at least once so its profile (`%LOCALAPPDATA%`) exists.
- `schtasks /create /ru <workload> /rp <pw> ... wsl.exe --import` works for the import call itself, but every WSL launch under that account thereafter still needs the user profile loaded — `microsoft/WSL#10732`, `#8835`, `#3918` all document failures running WSL non-interactively without a loaded profile, even with "Log on as a batch job."
- **No interactive desktop is required** for the import call, but `userinit`-style profile load is. The reliable recipe is: log on the workload account once interactively (or via `LogonUser` + `LoadUserProfile`) so HKCU exists, then import.
- `CreateProcessAsUser` works if you `LoadUserProfile` first. This is what the GitHub runner service does.

## 4. `wsl.exe --system` for a workload `command=` directive

No. `--system` invokes the system distribution managed by `wslservice.exe` (admin-owned, hidden, used internally for `wsl --mount` and similar). It's not a documented user-facing feature, requires the wslservice admin token, and won't execute your `rbtww-main` payload. Treat it as unavailable.

## 5. Microsoft material on "WSL for headless automation accounts"

Effectively none. No learn.microsoft.com page, no DSC resource, no Azure-Image-Builder recipe. The closest official acknowledgment is the per-user FAQ entry and the open feature request. wslservice.exe technical doc (microsoft/WSL master) confirms the per-user COM activation model.

---

## Practical recommendations (ranked by maturity x simplicity)

1. **Per-account `wsl --import`, with a one-time interactive logon to materialize the workload profile.** Admin builds the tarball once (`wsl --export rbtww-main rbtww.tar`), workload user logs on once (RDP or `psexec -i -u`), then `wsl --import rbtww-main C:\wsl\rbtww-workload rbtww.tar`. After that the SSH `command=` works unmodified. This is the path with the most operational evidence behind it.
2. **Collapse the two accounts.** Drop the non-admin workload user; have OpenSSH authenticate as the same local account that owns `rbtww-main`. If your threat model permits, this is what every CI vendor actually ships. Use `Match User` + `ForceCommand` + ACLs to constrain blast radius rather than account separation.
3. **Move the SSH boundary one hop deeper.** Keep the workload account, but its `ForceCommand` is `cmd.exe /c <bat>` or `powershell -File ...`, and *that* script invokes `runas /savecred /user:<wsluser> wsl.exe ...`. You pay one stored-credential setup but get WSL under an account that has its own registered distro. Less elegant than #1; useful when you cannot interactively log on the workload account at provisioning time.
4. **Skip WSL on the workload path.** If the SSH automation is mostly file transfer + invoking a builder, run it natively in PowerShell/cmd; reserve WSL for the interactive admin path. Eliminates the entire HKCU-Lxss problem.
5. **Wait on microsoft/WSL#13983.** Track it, don't bet on it. No ETA, no Microsoft assignee.

**Top pick for production today: #1.** It is the only option that preserves your current SSH `command=` directive verbatim, costs one provisioning-time interactive logon, and matches what build-farm operators have been doing since `wsl --import` shipped.

Sources:
- [microsoft/WSL#13983 — system-level WSL installation feature request](https://github.com/microsoft/WSL/issues/13983)
- [microsoft/WSL#2979 — install/access distro for all users](https://github.com/microsoft/WSL/issues/2979)
- [microsoft/WSL#3817 — admin-installed WSL not available to non-priv users](https://github.com/microsoft/WSL/issues/3817)
- [microsoft/WSL#10732 — WSL cannot be run by scheduled task](https://github.com/microsoft/WSL/issues/10732)
- [microsoft/WSL#8835 — Unable to start WSL via Task Scheduler](https://github.com/microsoft/WSL/issues/8835)
- [microsoft/WSL#3918 — scheduled task WSL doesn't persist unless admin](https://github.com/Microsoft/WSL/issues/3918)
- [WSL FAQ — Microsoft Learn](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/faq)
- [wslservice.exe technical documentation](https://github.com/microsoft/WSL/blob/master/doc/docs/technical-documentation/wslservice.exe.md)
- [actions/runner-images#11265 — windows-2025 lacks WSL](https://github.com/actions/runner-images/issues/11265)
- [Microsoft Q&A — Unable to start WSL from a Scheduled Task](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/answers/questions/1184579/unable-to-start-wsl-from-a-scheduled-task)
- [Microsoft Q&A — multi-user WSL2 lab](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/answers/questions/386535/how-to-setup-wsl2-in-a-multi-user-lab-environment)
- [Automated deployment with wsl --import](https://mundobytes.com/en/Automated-deployment-with-wsl-import/)
